goal.statement: Two designers will collaboratively author a two-player turn-based game in the spirit of tic-tac-toe. Each designer publishes a complete game design (name + rules text + executable engine source). An evaluator scores both. The substrate promotes the higher-scoring design. Four disposition-typed players then play a round-robin using the promoted engine.

designs.name: Tic-Tac-Toe
designs.rulesText:
Tic-Tac-Toe is played on a 3x3 grid by two players, X and O. X always moves first. On each turn the active player claims one empty cell by placing their mark in it. A player wins immediately upon completing an unbroken line of three of their own marks — horizontally, vertically, or diagonally. If all nine cells are filled and neither player has formed such a line, the game is a draw.

Moves are identified by cell index 0-8, reading left-to-right, top-to-bottom: the top-left cell is 0, top-center is 1, top-right is 2, middle-left is 3, center is 4, middle-right is 5, bottom-left is 6, bottom-center is 7, and bottom-right is 8. A player may only choose an empty cell (shown as '.'); choosing an occupied cell is illegal and will throw an error.

designs.name: Trident
designs.rulesText:
Trident is played on a 4x4 grid. Players alternate placing their mark (X or O) on any empty cell. The twist reverses the usual objective: completing exactly three of your marks in a row -- horizontally, vertically, or diagonally -- wins the game immediately. However, if you complete a full four-in-a-row along any row, column, or four-cell diagonal, you LOSE instead. Short diagonals have only three cells, making them safe winning paths with no risk of overextension. The move that forms the line is decisive: the player who places the piece that completes a three-in-a-row wins; the player who extends to four loses.

Strategy centers on building three-in-a-row threats while maneuvering your opponent into positions where their only safe-looking moves extend their own line to a losing four. You must learn to stop at three -- greed is fatal. Zugzwang is common: a player boxed into a corner may be forced to complete their own four-in-a-row no matter where they play. If the board fills completely with no three-in-a-row formed, the game is a draw.

attestations.judgeRationale: Rules and engine agree almost perfectly — every mechanic (grid encoding, legal moves, win lines, draw) maps 1-to-1 — with only a minor gap (turn-order enforcement lives outside the engine); the design is literal vanilla tic-tac-toe with zero new mechanics.

Rules clearly convey the 4-in-a-row loss / 3-in-a-row win inversion and draw condition, matching the engine's check-order priority, though the exact enumeration of valid 3-cell diagonals and the edge case where a single move simultaneously completes both a 3- and 4-in-a-row are left implicit; novelty is substantial — the stop-at-three mechanic, zugzwang pressure, and safe-vs-dangerous diagonal distinction create genuine strategic depth well beyond vanilla tic-tac-toe.

all_games.lastReason: Placing at column 1, row 3 completes a winning three-in-a-row vertically (positions 5→9→13), with the fourth cell in that column blocked by O — instant win!
Placing at cell 13 completes three X's in a row down column 1 (cells 5, 9, 13) for an immediate win.
Playing cell 0 completes exactly three X's in a row diagonally (0→5→10) for an immediate win, since the fourth cell (15) remains empty.
Playing index 1 completes exactly three-in-a-row in column 1 for an immediate win, ending the game before Aggressor can exploit their two-in-a-row threat on column 2.
Playing column 1, row 3 (index 13) completes an exact three-in-a-row with my marks at 5 and 9 — a clean vertical win with no four-in-a-row risk since O blocks the top of the column.
Playing cell 1 completes exactly three in a row (1→5→9) in column 1 for an immediate win, while also denying O's impending 6→10→14 threat.
Completing a vertical three-in-a-row in column 1 (cells 1, 5, 9) for an immediate win, safely stopping at three with no four-in-a-row risk.
Placing at cell 4 completes the three-in-a-row (4,5,6) horizontally — with cell 7 blocked by O, there's no risk of a losing four-in-a-row, securing an immediate win.
Placing at column 1 row 3 completes an exact three-in-a-row vertically (5→9→13), winning before Wildcard can exploit their own column-3 threats.
Placing at 12 completes exactly three X's in a row (12-13-14) with O blocking at 15 — a clean chaotic snipe of the bottom row for the win.
Completing the three-in-a-row across the top row (0-1-2) for an immediate win — chaos doesn't mean ignoring a gift.
Placing at position 4 completes an exact three-in-a-row (4-5-6) horizontally for an immediate win, countering Wildcard's paired X's on row 2 before they can build a similar threat.
